elements.sent-2411-002: Raine, Lecture Notes in Computer Science, Springer, volume 8369, pages~286-295, 2014.. Edition May 2023.
elements.sent-2411-003: In this contribution, we construct a connection between two quantum voting models presented previously.
elements.sent-2411-004: We propose to try to determine the result of a vote from associated given opinion polls.
elements.sent-2411-005: We introduce a density operator relative to the family of all candidates to a particular election.
elements.sent-2411-006: From an hypothesis of proportionality between a family of coefficients which characterize the density matrix and the probabilities of vote for all the candidates, we propose a numerical method for the entire determination of the density operator.
elements.sent-2411-007: This approach is a direct consequence of the Perron-Frobenius theorem for irreductible positive matrices.
elements.sent-2411-008: We apply our algorithm to synthetic data and to operational results issued from the French presidential election of April 2012.
elements.sent-2411-009: We keep in mind that opinion polls are intrinsically complex (see {\it e.g. Gallup \cite{Ga44 ) and give an approximates picture of a possible social reality.}}
elements.sent-2411-010: They are traditionnally of two types: popularity polls for various outstanding political personnalities and voting intention polls when a list of candidates is known.
elements.sent-2411-011: We have two different informations and to construct a link between them is not an easy task.
elements.sent-2411-012: In particular, the determination of the voting intentions is a quasi intractable problem! Predictions of votes classically use of so-called ``voting functions''.
elements.sent-2411-013: Voting functions have been developed for the prediction of presidential elections in the United States.
elements.sent-2411-014: They are based on correlations between economical parameters, popularity polls and other technical parameters.
elements.sent-2411-015: We refer to Abramowitz \cite {Ab88 , Lewis-Beck \cite {LB91 , Campbell \cite{Ca92 and Lafay \cite{La97 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-016: They conduct to present-day researches like range voting, independently proposed by Balinski and Laraki \cite{BL07a, BL07b and by Rivest and Smith \cite {Sm2k, RS07 .}}
elements.sent-2411-017: It is composed by two steps: grading and ranking.
elements.sent-2411-018: In the grading step, all the candidates are evaluated by all the electors.
elements.sent-2411-019: This first step is quite analogous to a popularity investigations and we will merge the two notions in this contribution.
elements.sent-2411-020: The second step of range voting is a majority ranking; it consists of a successive extraction of medians.
elements.sent-2411-021: The fact of considering quantum modelling induces a specific vision of probabilities.
elements.sent-2411-022: We refer {\it e.g. to the classical treatise on quantum mechanics of Cohen-Tannoudji {\it et al. \cite{CDL77 , to the so-called contextual objectivity proposed by Grangier \cite{Gr02 , or to the elementary introduction proposed by Busemeyer and Trueblood \cite {BT09 in the context of statistical inference.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-023: In Section~5, we develop the main idea of this paper.
elements.sent-2411-024: We construct a link between opinion polls and voting.
elements.sent-2411-025: This idea is tested numerically in Sections~6 and~7 for synthetic data and a ``real life'' election.
